pred_reason: The labels SWC-101 (Integer Overflow and Underflow) and SWC-107 (Reentrancy) are reasonable due to identified integer overflow risks in totalSupply calculations and multiple reentrancy vulnerabilities in the Release function. Evidence: Potential overflow in totalSupply() when multiplying large WETH balances by 1 billion (SWC-101); Release function makes external calls before state changes, violating Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern (SWC-107); Cross-function reentrancy possibilities between Release() and EncryptedSwap() functions (SWC-107)

findings.Description: The Release function makes an external call before completing state changes, creating a reentrancy vulnerability. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Potential complete drainage of all tokens controlled by this contract through recursive calls.

findings.Description: The Release function violates the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern by making external calls before state changes. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Could lead to reentrancy vulnerabilities if state changes are added after external calls in future modifications.

findings.Description: Potential cross-function reentrancy issues exist due to unsafe external call patterns and inconsistent operation ordering. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Could lead to inconsistent balance states or unauthorized transfers if combined with other vulnerabilities.

findings.Description: The totalSupply function could overflow when multiplying large WETH balances by 1 billion. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Could break functionality relying on accurate total supply calculations and lead to incorrect accounting.

findings.Issue: Missing Access Controls on ERC20 Functions
findings.Description: Core ERC20 functions (approve, transfer, transferFrom) lack the BotPower modifier applied to other sensitive functions. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Could allow unauthorized token transfers or approvals, violating intended contract logic.

findings.Issue: Privilege Escalation Between Bot and Keeper Roles
findings.Description: Both bot and keeper roles can modify each other's addresses, creating circular privilege escalation. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Compromise of either role could lead to full contract takeover.

findings.Description: SafeMath functions are not consistently used throughout the contract, particularly in EncryptedSwap. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Potential arithmetic overflows/underflows leading to incorrect calculations.

findings.Description: Contract initialization via constructor is vulnerable to front-running attacks. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Attacker could hijack control of the contract during deployment.

findings.Description: Unlimited approval patterns and privileged transfer functions create MEV opportunities. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Could lead to front-running attacks and financial losses for users.

findings.Description: The EncryptedSwap function requires admin privileges but performs transfers that shouldn't necessarily need them. This issue appears in the smart contract code.
findings.Impact: Could allow admins to arbitrarily transfer tokens without proper checks.
